walkthrough.title: What's in the Box
walkthrough.text: Let's get Spyfall set up. Open the box and you'll find 240 cards organized into 30 decks of 8 cards each. Each deck represents a different location, like a casino, a pirate ship, or a space station. You'll also find 30 ziplock bags (one per deck) and this rulebook. Before your first game, you'll need to sort the cards into their location decks, so let's do that now.

walkthrough.title: Sort Cards into Location Decks
walkthrough.text: Each location deck has seven cards with identical illustrations showing the location and one spy card. The seven location cards each show a different role at that location. For example, the Crusader Army deck has a Knight, a Squire, an Archer, a Monk, a Servant, a Bishop, and an Imprisoned Saracen, plus one spy card. Sort all 30 decks so each one has its seven matching location cards and one spy card.

walkthrough.title: Bag the Decks
walkthrough.text: Once each deck is sorted, place it face down into one of the 30 ziplock bags. Make sure the spy card is on top of each deck, face down. This matters because of how the dealing works later. Every card should be face down so nobody accidentally sees a location or role before the game starts. Pop each bag into the box when it's ready.

walkthrough.title: Study the Location List
walkthrough.text: Before your first game, everyone should take a look at the location list in the middle spread of the rulebook. There are 30 locations in total, including places like Airplane, Bank, Beach, Casino, Circus Tent, Crusader Army, Day Spa, Embassy, Hospital, Hotel, Military Base, Movie Studio, Ocean Liner, Pirate Ship, Polar Station, Police Station, Restaurant, School, Space Station, Submarine, Supermarket, Theater, University, and more. This is important because when someone is the spy, they'll need to guess from this list. Study it now so you don't have to peek during the game. That's a dead giveaway.

walkthrough.title: Grab a Timer
walkthrough.text: You'll need a stopwatch or timer for each round. Someone's phone works perfectly. The standard round length is 8 minutes, but for your first few games, you might want to play 12 to 15 minute rounds to give everyone more time to get comfortable with the question-and-answer flow. Decide on your round length before you start.

walkthrough.title: Decide How Many Rounds to Play
walkthrough.text: Before the first round, agree on how many rounds you'll play. The rulebook recommends five rounds for your first session, which should take about an hour. You can play more or fewer depending on your group. Points are tallied after each round, and whoever has the most points after all rounds wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Dealer
walkthrough.text: Now pick your first dealer. The rulebook says the most suspicious-looking player deals first, which is a fun way to start. The dealer plays the game as a regular player too, so everyone is always involved. In future rounds, the player who was the spy in the previous round becomes the next dealer.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Select a Location Deck
walkthrough.text: The dealer takes all 30 ziplock bags out of the box and turns them face down. Give them a good shuffle so nobody knows which location is which. The dealer then picks one random bag without peeking. Very carefully remove the cards from the selected bag, making sure not to flip any over. The location for this round is now set.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Cards
walkthrough.text: The dealer counts out a number of cards from the top of the deck equal to the number of players. If you have five players, take the top five cards. Remember, the spy card was placed on top, so it will always be in the mix. Shuffle these selected cards together, then deal one face down to each player. The remaining cards in the deck are set aside without being revealed. Nobody looks at the undealt cards.

walkthrough.title: Look at Your Card
walkthrough.text: Each player now secretly looks at their card without letting anyone else see it. If your card shows a location name and a role, you're one of the non-spy players. You know where you are and what your role is at that location. If your card is the spy card, you don't know the location. Your mission is to figure out where everyone is without giving yourself away. Place your card face down in front of you once you've memorized it. You're ready to play!

Location card: you know the location and your role

Place card face down in front of you

walkthrough.title: Starting the Round
walkthrough.text: Once everyone has looked at their card, the dealer starts the stopwatch and the round begins. The dealer kicks things off by asking one of the other players a question, calling them by name. Something like, 'Tell me, John...' and then asking a question. Questions usually relate to the current location, but they don't have to. This first question sets the tone for the entire round.

walkthrough.title: Asking and Answering Questions
walkthrough.text: Here's how the question flow works. You ask one question to one player. That player gives an answer, which can take any form — serious, cryptic, joking, evasive. Then the player who just answered gets to ask the next question to any other player, except they can't ask the person who just asked them. No follow-up questions allowed. One question per turn, and the chain continues. The order is entirely up to the players, based on their suspicions.

walkthrough.title: The Non-Spy's Goal
walkthrough.text: If you're a non-spy, you have two objectives. First, prove to the other non-spies that you know the location by giving subtle hints in your questions and answers. Second, figure out who the spy is. The tricky part is that your hints need to be oblique enough that the spy can't figure out the location from them. For example, if you're at the Beach and someone asks what you did yesterday, saying 'I went swimming in the ocean' is way too obvious. The spy would know you're at the beach instantly. Something like 'I enjoyed the weather' is much better — vague enough to keep the spy guessing.

walkthrough.title: The Spy's Goal
walkthrough.text: If you got the spy card, your job is to blend in. Listen very carefully to every question and answer. The other players will be dropping subtle hints about the location, and you need to piece those hints together without revealing that you have no idea where you are. Your ultimate goal is to either survive the full round without being caught, or identify the location before time runs out. Both are winning conditions for you.

walkthrough.title: Using Your Role (Optional Rule)
walkthrough.text: Each non-spy player's card also shows a specific role at the location. Before the game, your group should agree on whether to play with roles or not. If you play with roles, you should stay in character when answering questions. For example, if you're the Student at the University and someone asks about your daily routine, you might mention classes and studying. The rulebook strongly recommends using roles to make the game more interesting, but suggests skipping them for your first few sessions while everyone learns the basics. Roles are always gender-neutral, by the way — a flight attendant can be male or female.

walkthrough.title: Accusing a Player During the Round
walkthrough.text: At any point during the round while the clock is running, any player may stop the clock to accuse another player of being the spy. This is a once-per-round ability for each player. When you stop the clock, name the suspect and ask all other players to vote. The suspect does not get a vote. If every single other player unanimously agrees, the suspect's card is revealed. If the vote is not unanimous, restart the clock and keep playing. The accuser loses their ability to stop the clock for the rest of the round, but can still make an accusation after time runs out. The same person can be accused multiple times by different players.

walkthrough.title: When Time Runs Out
walkthrough.text: If the eight minutes run out without anyone being caught, an accusation phase begins. Starting with the dealer, each player in turn gets to accuse one other player of being the spy. Players can now openly discuss their suspicions. If all players unanimously agree with an accusation (the accused doesn't vote), that player's card is revealed. If no unanimous vote is reached, the accuser passes to the player on their left. If every player has had a chance to accuse and no one has been unanimously convicted, the spy wins. A player can be targeted by multiple accusers during this phase.

walkthrough.title: The Spy's Secret Weapon
walkthrough.text: The spy has one powerful move: at any time while the clock is running (and hasn't been stopped by another player's accusation), the spy can reveal their spy card, stop the clock, and attempt to guess the location. The spy consults the location list in the rulebook and names one location. If they're right, the spy wins. If they're wrong, the non-spy players win. This is a huge risk-reward play. If you've been piecing clues together and feel confident, go for it. But once you reveal your spy card, there's no going back.

walkthrough.title: Important Voting Rule
walkthrough.text: Here's a critical rule that's easy to forget: during voting on a suspect, players should NOT discuss their reasons for thinking someone is the spy. Why? Because your arguments could accidentally reveal the location to the spy. If someone says 'I think she's the spy because she didn't know about the operating room,' the spy just learned you might be at the Hospital. Keep your voting arguments general — 'She seemed evasive' is safer than revealing location-specific details.

walkthrough.title: Scoring the Round
walkthrough.text: Once the round ends, tally up points. If the spy won by surviving without anyone being accused, the spy earns 2 points. If a non-spy was wrongly accused, the spy earns 4 points. If the spy revealed themselves and correctly guessed the location, the spy earns 4 points. On the flip side, if the non-spies successfully caught the spy, each non-spy earns 1 point, except for the player who initiated the successful accusation — that player earns 2 points instead.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Cleanup
walkthrough.text: After scoring, collect all the cards from this round and return them to the dealer. This deck is now retired for the rest of the game — put the ziplock bag back in the box. You won't use the same location twice in one game session. The player who was the spy this round becomes the dealer for the next round. They pick a new random bag and deal as before.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: After the agreed-upon number of rounds (five for your first session), add up everyone's total points across all rounds. The player with the most points wins the game. Since points come from both spy and non-spy rounds, the best players are good at both roles. Someone who is a fantastic spy but gets caught easily as a non-spy will struggle just as much as someone who catches spies but can never bluff their way through a spy round.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Practice Round
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a scripted practice round with five players: Alex, Blake, Casey, Dana, and Eli. This is a great example player count for Spyfall — enough people for interesting discussion without being overwhelming. We'll use the Passenger Train location for this example. Follow along to see how questions, answers, accusations, and scoring all work in practice.

walkthrough.title: Dealing the Cards
walkthrough.text: Alex is the dealer. They shuffle the ziplock bags face down, grab a random one, and carefully remove the cards. Since there are five players, Alex takes the top five cards from the deck, shuffles them, and deals one to each player. The remaining three cards go aside, face down. Nobody touches them.

walkthrough.title: Checking Your Cards
walkthrough.text: Everyone sneaks a peek at their card. Alex sees 'Passenger Train — Restaurant Chef.' Blake sees 'Passenger Train — Mechanic.' Casey sees 'Passenger Train — Border Patrol.' Dana gets the spy card — no location, no role, just 'SPY.' Eli sees 'Passenger Train — Passenger.' Dana's heart rate jumps, but they keep a straight face. The round is about to begin.

walkthrough.title: The First Question
walkthrough.text: Alex starts the timer and asks: 'Blake, how do you feel about the food around here?' This is a great opening question. It's related to the Passenger Train (trains have dining cars) but vague enough that it could apply to many locations. Blake, the Mechanic, answers: 'Honestly? It's okay, but I don't spend much time thinking about food. I'm usually busy with the equipment.' Solid answer — hints at a work role without being specific about the location.

walkthrough.title: The Chain Continues
walkthrough.text: Blake now asks Casey a question (Blake can't ask Alex, who just asked them). Blake says: 'Casey, do you see a lot of new faces around here?' Casey, the Border Patrol agent, answers: 'Oh definitely. New people coming and going all the time. It's my job to keep an eye on them.' This is a nice in-character answer. Casey is hinting at their role without saying 'I check passports on trains.' Meanwhile, Dana the spy is taking mental notes: food, equipment, new faces, keeping an eye on people... could be a hotel? A restaurant? An airport?

walkthrough.title: The Spy Gets Asked
walkthrough.text: Casey asks Dana: 'Dana, is this your first time here?' Dana doesn't know the location, so they go vague: 'No, I've been here a few times before. It's always a different experience though.' This is a safe spy answer — generic enough to work for any location. The other players make note that Dana's answer is a bit bland, but nothing suspicious enough to call them out yet.

walkthrough.title: The Spy Asks a Fishing Question
walkthrough.text: Now Dana needs to ask someone a question. This is where the spy does their detective work. Dana asks Eli: 'Eli, what's the best part about being here?' Eli, the Passenger, answers: 'I love watching the scenery go by. Very relaxing.' Dana's brain lights up. Scenery going by? That narrows it down quite a bit. Maybe a cruise ship, a passenger train, or an airplane?

walkthrough.title: Suspicion Builds
walkthrough.text: Eli now asks Alex: 'Alex, have you been working here long?' Alex, the Restaurant Chef, answers: 'A couple of years. The kitchen keeps me busy, but I enjoy it.' Now the non-spy players all give subtle nods to each other — they're all clearly on the same page about the location. But Dana heard 'kitchen' and 'scenery going by' and is now pretty confident. An Ocean Liner? A Passenger Train? Dana decides to gather one more piece of information before making a move.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Being Too Specific
walkthrough.text: Let's pause the action to point out a mistake. When Alex said 'the kitchen keeps me busy,' that was a bit too specific. A spy who hears 'kitchen' plus 'scenery going by' can quickly narrow down the options. A better answer might have been: 'I stay busy in my area. There's always something to prepare.' The lesson here is that non-spy players should reveal just enough to prove they know the location, but not so much that they hand it to the spy on a silver platter.

walkthrough.title: The Second Round of Questions
walkthrough.text: Alex asks Dana: 'Dana, what do you think of the accommodations here?' Dana, still not 100% sure of the location, plays it safe: 'They're compact but functional. You get used to it.' This is clever spy work — 'compact but functional' works for a train, a submarine, a space station, or even a hotel room. But to the non-spy players, it feels just vague enough to be suspicious.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: The Spy Panicking
walkthrough.text: Here's another common mistake we want to highlight. Some new spies, when they feel the heat building, will suddenly change their behavior — getting quiet, giving one-word answers, or asking overly specific questions to pin down the location. This is a dead giveaway. If Dana had asked something like 'So how long does it usually take to get there?' that would scream spy. Stay calm, keep your answers at the same level of vagueness as everyone else, and don't let the pressure rush you into a mistake.

walkthrough.title: Someone Stops the Clock
walkthrough.text: About five minutes in, Blake stops the clock and accuses Dana: 'I think Dana is the spy. Their answers have been really generic, and they haven't mentioned anything location-specific.' Blake asks everyone to vote. Alex votes yes, Casey votes yes, Eli votes yes. Dana doesn't get a vote. It's unanimous! Dana's card is revealed: SPY. The non-spy players win!

walkthrough.title: Scoring This Round
walkthrough.text: Time to score. The non-spies won, so each non-spy player earns 1 point. But Blake, who made the successful accusation, earns 2 points instead of 1. The scores are: Alex 1, Blake 2, Casey 1, Dana 0, Eli 1. Dana earns nothing this round since they were caught.

walkthrough.title: What If the Spy Had Guessed?
walkthrough.text: Let's rewind and imagine an alternate ending. What if Dana had felt confident enough to stop the game before Blake's accusation? Dana would reveal the spy card and say: 'The location is... the Passenger Train.' If correct, Dana earns 4 points and nobody else scores. That's a massive swing — 4 points for the spy versus 0 for everyone else. This is why the spy's guess is such a powerful tool when used at the right moment.

walkthrough.title: What If the Vote Had Failed?
walkthrough.text: Let's also consider what happens if the vote against Dana wasn't unanimous. Say Eli had voted no. The clock would restart, Blake would lose their ability to stop the clock for the rest of the round, and the game would continue. Dana would still be under suspicion, but they'd have more time to either blend in or gather enough clues to guess the location. Failed votes are great for spies because they burn an accuser's one-time ability.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Accusing Too Early
walkthrough.text: Blake's accusation at five minutes was well-timed because multiple players had noticed Dana's vague answers. But a common beginner mistake is accusing someone on the first round of questions based on a single weird answer. If Blake had accused Dana after just one question, the other players might not have been ready to vote yes, and Blake would have wasted their accusation ability. Give it at least two rounds of questions before making a move, so you have enough evidence to convince everyone.

walkthrough.title: Setting Up the Next Round
walkthrough.text: The cards from this round go back into the Passenger Train bag, and that bag is retired for the rest of the game. Dana was the spy, so Dana becomes the dealer for the next round. Dana picks a new random bag, deals the cards, and a brand new round begins with a completely different location. Nobody knows who the spy will be this time.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations!
walkthrough.text: You've just walked through a complete round of Spyfall. You've seen how questions and answers flow, how the spy gathers clues, how non-spies give subtle hints, how accusations work, and how scoring plays out. You've also seen three common mistakes: being too specific with your answers, panicking as the spy, and accusing too early. Keep these in mind during your first real game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things to remember.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics that will sharpen both your spy and non-spy play.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all 30 locations and key game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun, and remember: loose lips sink ships!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips for spy and non-spy strategy
Reference the Appendix for all 30 locations and key terms
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: The Art of the Vague Answer
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in Spyfall is calibrating your vagueness. Whether you're a spy or a non-spy, your answers need to be in a sweet spot. Too specific, and the spy learns the location instantly. Too vague, and other players will suspect you of being the spy. Aim for answers that a person who knows the location would give, but that could also apply to two or three other locations. Practice thinking in categories. Instead of naming a specific object at a location, describe a feeling, a routine, or a general experience that fits.

walkthrough.title: Non-Spy Strategy: Prove Without Revealing
walkthrough.text: As a non-spy, you need to walk a tightrope. Your fellow non-spies are watching you for signs that you know the location. If you're too evasive, they'll think you're the spy. But you also can't just blurt out details because the real spy is listening. The trick is to make statements that are meaningfully connected to the location for people who know it, but ambiguous to someone who doesn't. Use your assigned role as inspiration. If you're the Chef at the Restaurant, talk about being 'busy during peak hours' rather than 'flipping burgers in the kitchen.'

walkthrough.title: Spy Strategy: Listen First, Talk Second
walkthrough.text: When you're the spy, your most powerful tool is your ears. Before you answer any question, you should be absorbing every word that other players say. Early in the round, keep your answers as neutral as possible. Something like 'I like it here well enough' or 'Things have been pretty normal lately' buys you time. As the round progresses and you pick up more clues, you can start making your answers slightly more specific to match the vibe of the conversation. The best spies sound natural because they've been absorbing context from everyone else.

walkthrough.title: Ask Questions That Test, Not Reveal
walkthrough.text: When it's your turn to ask a question, think about what information you'll get from the answer versus what information you're giving away. A great question is one where the answer will help you identify the spy, but the question itself doesn't narrow down the location. For example, 'How long have you been working here?' is a great question because a spy will struggle to answer convincingly regardless of location, while a non-spy can answer naturally based on their role. Avoid questions like 'What floor are we on?' which immediately tells the spy they're in a building.

walkthrough.title: Watch Body Language and Timing
walkthrough.text: Spyfall is a social game, and words aren't the only clue. Watch how long someone takes to answer. A spy who just got asked a tough question will often pause, look away, or stall for time. Meanwhile, non-spies usually answer quickly because they already know the location. Also watch for players who are visibly nervous, avoiding eye contact, or giving answers that sound rehearsed rather than natural. These are all classic spy tells.

walkthrough.title: The Spy's Guess: When to Pull the Trigger
walkthrough.text: As the spy, you have the option to reveal yourself and guess the location at any time. But when should you do it? Here's a good rule of thumb: if you're about 80% confident in the location and you can feel the group starting to close in on you, go for the guess. A correct guess is worth 4 points. Waiting too long and getting caught is worth 0. But guessing wrong also gives you 0 and hands the non-spies their victory. The worst-case scenario is getting caught AND not having narrowed it down enough to guess. So gather clues aggressively in the first few minutes.

walkthrough.title: The Accusation Economy
walkthrough.text: Each player can only stop the clock once per round. This means accusations are a limited resource. Don't waste yours on a hunch. Wait until you've seen a pattern — two or three suspicious answers from the same person. And before you stop the clock, quietly gauge whether the other players seem to share your suspicion. An accusation that fails because one person voted no is a wasted opportunity, and it burns your ability to stop the clock for the rest of the round. Time your accusations carefully.

walkthrough.title: Spies Can Accuse Too
walkthrough.text: Here's a pro tip that new players often miss: the spy can accuse other players. This is a powerful misdirection tool. If you're the spy and someone is getting suspicious of you, consider stopping the clock and accusing someone else. Even if the vote fails, you've redirected attention away from yourself and burned your 'I accused someone' move, which can actually make you seem more innocent. It's risky, but in the right situation, it can completely throw the group off your trail.

summary.title: The Art of the Vague Answer
bullets: Calibrate vagueness: too specific helps the spy, too vague makes you look like the spy
Aim for answers that fit 2-3 locations
Think in categories: feelings, routines, general experiences

summary.title: Non-Spy Strategy: Prove Without Revealing
bullets: Prove you know the location to fellow non-spies
Use your role for inspiration without stating it directly
Statements should be meaningful to insiders, ambiguous to the spy

summary.title: Spy Strategy: Listen First, Talk Second
bullets: Absorb every word before forming your answers
Start neutral: 'Things have been pretty normal lately'
Gradually match the conversation's vibe as you pick up clues

summary.title: Ask Questions That Test, Not Reveal
bullets: Good questions help identify the spy without narrowing the location
'How long have you been working here?' = good (tests role knowledge)
'What floor are we on?' = bad (tells spy you're in a building)

summary.title: Watch Body Language and Timing
bullets: Spies often pause or stall when asked tough questions
Non-spies answer quickly because they know the location
Watch for nervous behavior, rehearsed-sounding answers, and eye contact avoidance

summary.title: The Spy's Guess: When to Pull the Trigger
bullets: Guess at ~80% confidence if the group is closing in on you
Correct guess = 4 points; getting caught = 0
Gather clues aggressively in the first few minutes
Waiting too long risks getting caught before you can guess

summary.title: The Accusation Economy
bullets: Each player can stop the clock once per round — don't waste it
Wait for 2-3 suspicious answers before accusing
Gauge group consensus before stopping the clock
A failed accusation burns your ability for the round

summary.title: Spies Can Accuse Too
bullets: Spy can stop the clock and accuse someone else as misdirection
Redirects attention and can make the spy seem more innocent
Risky but powerful in the right situation

walkthrough.title: The Chameleon Spy
walkthrough.text: Advanced spies don't just give vague answers — they actively construct a believable persona. When you pick up enough clues to narrow the location down to two or three possibilities, start acting as if you're at the most likely one. Choose an imaginary role and commit to it. If you think you're at the Hospital, start answering as if you're a nurse or a patient. If someone catches an inconsistency, you can always pivot, but most of the time a spy who commits to a character is far harder to catch than one who stays permanently vague.

walkthrough.title: The Question Redirect
walkthrough.text: When someone asks you a question as the spy, you can buy yourself time and gather intel by partially answering and then quickly redirecting. For example: 'That's a good question. Honestly, things have been pretty quiet on my end. But speaking of quiet, Casey, have you noticed anything unusual today?' This technique shifts attention away from you and forces another player to provide more clues. Just don't do it too obviously or too often, or people will notice the pattern.

walkthrough.title: Location Elimination as the Spy
walkthrough.text: Expert spies don't just listen for what the location is — they actively eliminate what it isn't. Keep a mental list of the 30 locations and cross them off based on clues. If someone mentions outdoor weather, you can eliminate Submarine, Space Station, and Movie Studio. If someone mentions customers, that rules out Military Base, Crusader Army, and Polar Station. By mid-round, a sharp spy can usually narrow it down to two or three options, which gives them a good shot at guessing correctly if they need to.

walkthrough.title: The Trap Question
walkthrough.text: As a non-spy, you can set a trap for the spy by asking a question that sounds general but has a specific correct answer for the current location. For example, if you're at the Pirate Ship, you might ask: 'What did you have to drink last night?' A non-spy who knows they're on a pirate ship might say something about rum or grog. A spy who thinks they're at a Restaurant might say 'wine.' The key is that your question should seem innocent to the spy while being loaded with location-specific meaning for the other non-spies.

walkthrough.title: Playing the Meta Game
walkthrough.text: After several rounds, players start to learn each other's tells. Maybe Blake always gives long answers when they're the spy, or Casey gets weirdly quiet. Pay attention to these patterns across rounds, not just within them. Conversely, if you know your own tells, work to break them. Give long answers sometimes when you're not the spy. Be quiet sometimes when you know the location. The best Spyfall players are unpredictable — they vary their play style so that no one round reveals a reliable pattern.

walkthrough.title: The Non-Spy Alliance
walkthrough.text: In games with six or more players, the non-spies have a significant numerical advantage. Use it. If you're confident another player knows the location (because their answers have been subtly correct), start building an implicit alliance. Direct your questions to players you're less sure about. The more non-spies who confirm each other early, the more pressure falls on the remaining unverified players — which often includes the spy. In larger groups, the spy's main strategy should be hiding within the verified group as quickly as possible.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Optimization Over Multiple Rounds
walkthrough.text: Spyfall is scored across multiple rounds, and smart players think about the long game. Being the spy is high-variance: 0 points if caught, 2 to 4 if you win. Being a non-spy is low-variance: 1 point per win, 2 if you make the accusation. Over five rounds, the player who consistently earns 1-2 points as a non-spy and occasionally scores big as the spy will usually win. Don't take unnecessary risks as the spy just to go for 4 points if a safe 2 points (running out the clock) is achievable.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Accusation Timing
walkthrough.text: The endgame accusation phase after time runs out is often where games are decided. If you're a non-spy and the clock is about to expire, consider whether it's better to stop the clock now or let time run out. Stopping the clock early uses your one-time ability and requires an immediate vote. Letting time run out gives you the structured accusation phase where every player gets to accuse in order, with open discussion allowed. Sometimes it's strategically better to save your accusation for the post-timer phase where you can build a group consensus rather than rushing a vote that might fail.

summary.title: The Chameleon Spy
bullets: Construct a believable persona once you narrow down the location
Pick an imaginary role and commit to it
A spy who plays a character is harder to catch than one who stays vague

summary.title: The Question Redirect
bullets: Partially answer, then redirect with your own question
Shifts attention away from you and gathers more intel
Don't overuse — it becomes a detectable pattern

summary.title: Location Elimination as the Spy
bullets: Actively cross off impossible locations from the list of 30
Outdoor clues eliminate Submarine, Space Station, etc.
Customer clues eliminate Military Base, Crusader Army, etc.
By mid-round, narrow to 2-3 options for a strong guess

summary.title: The Trap Question
bullets: Ask questions that sound general but have location-specific correct answers
Example at Pirate Ship: 'What did you drink last night?' (rum vs. wine)
Loaded for non-spies, innocent-looking to the spy

summary.title: Playing the Meta Game
bullets: Learn other players' tells across multiple rounds
Break your own patterns — vary your play style when spy and non-spy
The best players are unpredictable

summary.title: The Non-Spy Alliance
bullets: In 6+ player games, non-spies have a big numerical advantage
Verify each other early, then focus questions on unverified players
Spy's goal in large groups: hide within the verified group quickly

summary.title: Scoring Optimization Over Multiple Rounds
bullets: Think across all rounds, not just the current one
Consistent 1-2 points as non-spy + occasional spy wins = victory
Don't risk 0 points chasing 4 when a safe 2 (running out clock) is reachable

summary.title: Advanced Accusation Timing
bullets: Consider saving your accusation for the post-timer phase
Post-timer allows open discussion and structured accusations
Early clock-stop requires immediate vote that might fail
Sometimes patience is more powerful than urgency

entries.definition: The card dealt to one player each round. It has no location or role — only the word 'SPY.' The spy does not know where anyone is and must deduce the location from other players' questions and answers.
A card showing the current round's location name and the player's assigned role at that location. All non-spy players receive one of these. The card tells you exactly where you are and who you are.

Each location card assigns a specific role to the player (e.g., Knight, Mechanic, Passenger). Players may optionally play in character. Roles are gender-neutral. The group decides before the game whether to use roles.

The player who shuffles the ziplock bags, selects a random deck, and deals cards. The dealer also plays as a regular player. The most suspicious-looking player deals first; after that, the previous round's spy deals.
A single game of Spyfall lasting up to 8 minutes (adjustable). Each round uses one location deck. A full game consists of an agreed number of rounds, typically five.
Used to track the 8-minute round length. Can be stopped by a player making an accusation or by the spy revealing their card. The timer is not included in the box — use a phone.

Any player may stop the clock once per round to accuse another player of being the spy. Requires a unanimous vote from all other players (the accused does not vote). If unanimous, the accused reveals their card.

When 8 minutes expire, an accusation phase begins. Starting with the dealer, each player gets to accuse someone. Players can openly discuss. Unanimous vote reveals the accused. If no one is convicted after all players have accused, the spy wins.
An accusation only succeeds if every player except the accused votes in favor. Even one dissenting vote means the accusation fails. The accused player never gets to vote on their own behalf.
The spy may reveal their spy card at any time while the clock is running (not stopped) and attempt to name the current location. Correct guess = spy wins (4 points). Wrong guess = non-spies win.

The flow of questions during a round. The questioner asks one player, that player answers and then asks someone else. You cannot ask the person who just asked you. No follow-up questions. Order is based on suspicion.
Each player asks exactly one question per turn. You cannot ask a follow-up or a second question. After the answer, the questioned player becomes the questioner.
If the round ends and no player is successfully accused of being the spy (after the post-timer accusation phase), the spy wins and earns 2 points.
If a non-spy player is unanimously accused and revealed, the spy wins and earns 4 points. This is the spy's best outcome.
If the spy reveals their card and correctly names the location, the spy earns 4 points and no one else scores.
If the spy is successfully accused, each non-spy player earns 1 point. The player who initiated the successful accusation earns 2 points instead of 1.
